output:
**[Question 1] - What is the problem?**

Does the practice of overtraining small Llama-3-style models far past Chinchilla-optimal on a fixed data mixture to trade training compute for inference efficiency rest on the hidden assumption that overtraining returns are capability-homogeneous and mixture-invariant, when distinct capabilities such as knowledge, multilingual memorization, reasoning, coding, and tool-use demonstrably saturate at different token budgets, so that the aggregate-loss-optimal overtraining point is downstream-capability-suboptimal and must be replaced by a capability-resolved data mixture whose composition is a function of the overtraining ratio r = D/D_Chinchilla under an inference-aware objective?

**[Question 2] - Why is it interesting and important?**

The field runs on a single default decision variable, and this idea attacks it. From Kaplan et al. (2020) through Chinchilla (Hoffmann et al., 2022) to the inference-aware law of Sardana et al. (2023), the operating frontier is fit to one scalar - aggregate next-token loss - measured on a fixed data mixture. Llama 3's own justification for overtraining its 8B model far past compute-optimal is exactly this argument: aggregate loss keeps falling, so trade training compute for inference efficiency. That single lens is not a niche detail; it is the variable practitioners use to pick token budgets, to decide how long to overtrain, and to compare a densely overtrained small model against a distilled one. Because almost every team now ships a small model, the aggregate-loss default governs an enormous and growing slice of real practice.

The hidden assumption inside that default is that overtraining returns are capability-homogeneous and mixture-invariant - that one more token helps all capabilities about equally, and that the best mixture does not move as you overtrain. The structural fact is the opposite: capabilities saturate at different token budgets. Knowledge and multilingual memorization are lookup-like and flatten relatively early; reasoning, coding, and tool-use keep improving deep into the overtrained regime. So at high overtraining ratio r = D/D_Chinchilla, extra tokens spent on already-saturated, high-frequency buckets still buy aggregate-loss reduction (those buckets dominate the average) while contributing little downstream, and the still-improving capabilities are starved. Aggregate loss and downstream capability dissociate. This is precisely the regime where the Gadre et al. (2024) finding that perplexity reliably predicts downstream - a result they establish under a fixed mixture - stops underwriting good decisions, because the moment you let the mixture move (the whole point of optimizing it), low aggregate loss no longer certifies a good capability profile.

The payoff is a genuine free lunch under the field's own constraints. Hold parameters fixed (same inference FLOPs per token, same memory, same on-device footprint), hold total training tokens fixed (same training budget), and re-allocate only which tokens are spent - the mixture as a function of r. The prediction is equal-or-worse aggregate validation loss but better aggregate downstream capability and, importantly, better worst-case (most-starved) capability. That is the Llama 3 objective - trade training compute for inference efficiency - done correctly: you stop paying for aggregate-loss reduction that does not convert into capability. For anyone shipping small or on-device models, this is the difference between a model that is uniformly mediocre and one that is balanced across the capabilities users actually invoke, at zero extra serving cost.

It also adds a knob the field currently lacks. Today the only lever the overtraining playbook exposes is scalar: train longer on the same mixture (choose r). The mixture is treated as a fixed hyperparameter, selected once at compute-optimal scale and frozen through overtraining. We make capability-targeted token allocation an explicit function of r, derived from per-capability saturation laws under an inference-aware objective. That converts mixture design from a one-shot pre-training choice into a scheduled control variable tied to how far you intend to overtrain - something the current frontier has no vocabulary for.

Even if a better operator for setting the mixture later appears, the contribution is durable, because it ships two things the field will keep. First, a falsifiable reframing: the inference-aware scaling law is capability-blind, and aggregate loss is the wrong sufficient statistic in the overtrained regime. Second, a reusable object - the per-capability saturation law - that future small-model reports can be required to measure and compare against. This mirrors how rate-distortion theory reframed compression: once you accept that bits must be scored against a distortion measure, you cannot go back to reporting a single scalar. Per-capability saturation curves are the distortion axis for overtraining; they turn "we lowered loss" into an incomplete claim.

One responsible-AI caveat travels with the result: cheaper-to-serve, more-capable small models are dual-use, and any mixture-allocation policy encodes representation and coverage priorities - especially across languages, since multilingual memorization is one of the early-saturating buckets we explicitly reallocate - so the policy must be audited to ensure "inference-optimal" does not quietly come to mean "deprioritize low-resource languages"; the Q5 optimization therefore imposes p_multilingual >= p_floor (its compute-optimal share at r=1) as an explicit constraint, so de-emphasis of low-resource languages is bounded rather than open-ended. Net: the idea targets a default decision variable used by nearly every team shipping a small model, shows that default systematically mis-spends training tokens in the regime everyone is now operating in, and replaces it with a budget-neutral, falsifiable, and reusable alternative.

**[Question 3] - Why is it hard?**

The first obstacle is a chicken-and-egg entanglement between the data mixture and the very notion of "saturation": saturation is not a property of a capability, it is a property of a capability GIVEN a mixture. The smaller Llama-3 recipe overtrains on a FIXED mixture, and the tempting fix is to "sort domains by how fast their capability saturates and pour the overtraining budget into the slow ones." This naive per-domain sorting fails because the axes are coupled. Raising the code proportion p_code so that coding keeps improving past a trillion tokens necessarily lowers the effective per-capability token count seen by knowledge/QA and multilingual, which shifts THEIR saturation points; the instant you reallocate to help capability A you have moved the saturation curve of B. Tokens-to-saturation is therefore a fixed-point quantity, not a lookup-table constant. The instrument that makes this tractable is a parametric JOINT saturation law L_c(N, D, {p_d}) -- per-capability loss as a function of model size N, total tokens D, and the full mixture vector p -- fit on a small ladder and solved by ALTERNATING (block-coordinate) optimization that iterates between (a) solving the mixture under the current fitted laws and (b) refitting the laws at the new mixture until the fixed point converges. The functional form borrows the Data Mixing Laws construction (Ye et al. 2024), which models loss as an exponential-family function of domain proportions, composed with the Chinchilla N,D power law (Hoffmann et al. 2022); that composition is exactly what lets us predict the saturation point of B AFTER probability mass moves toward A, instead of pretending it stood still.

The second obstacle is that the regime we care about -- r = D/D_Chinchilla of roughly 10x to 40x past compute-optimal -- is precisely where power-law fits are least validated. Chinchilla and Kaplan laws were calibrated near compute-optimal; far-overtrained behavior is contaminated by memorization, grokking (Power et al. 2022), epoch-wise double descent (Nakkiran et al. 2019), and apparent emergence (Wei et al. 2022, contested by Schaeffer et al. 2023), any of which can break a monotone power-law extrapolation. A naive "fit Chinchilla on a few near-optimal points and extrapolate to r=40" can be wrong by a large margin, and the error is silent -- nothing in the fit warns you. The instrument is a discipline of HELD-OUT EXTRAPOLATION validation: fit each law only on r <= k and score its prediction at r > k, carrying bootstrap confidence intervals on the fitted exponents so uncertainty travels with the prediction; and, critically, a SYNTHETIC-INJECTION CALIBRATION -- inject a synthetic skill with a KNOWN saturation token-budget (for example a templated formal-language or arithmetic task mixed in at a controlled rate) and verify the fitted law recovers that known budget before trusting it on real capabilities. This converts "do power laws even hold out here?" from an assumption into a measured, falsifiable check.

The third obstacle is definitional: you cannot cleanly count "reasoning tokens" or "knowledge tokens," because web text is unlabeled and skills are entangled within a single document. Any method that attributes loss to capabilities at the TOKEN level is building on sand. We sidestep this by operationalizing a capability not as a token label but as a held-out downstream EVAL CLUSTER, and by defining the mixture only over CONTROLLABLE DATA SOURCES whose proportions we actually set. Capabilities are measured by benchmark clusters -- knowledge/QA, multilingual, math/reasoning, code, commonsense -- while the optimization variables are the proportions p over corpora we can dial directly: a general-web corpus, a code corpus, a multilingual corpus, and a math/reasoning-heavy corpus. This decoupling -- controllable sources on the input side, benchmark clusters on the measurement side, joined by a fitted loss-to-accuracy link -- means we never need a token-level capability labeler; we need only proportions we control and benchmarks we can run.

The fourth obstacle is causal attribution: even if a capability-resolved mixture beats the fixed-mixture overtraining baseline, the gain might come from generic mixture tuning or a regularization side-effect, NOT from the named mechanism (saturation-aware reallocation). A single positive result cannot separate "our mechanism works" from "almost any reallocation of this magnitude helps." The instrument is a MECHANISM-CLAIM PLACEBO PAIR with pre-registered predictions: (i) a RANDOM-PARTITION / random-reallocation placebo that perturbs the mixture by the SAME magnitude but along a capability-agnostic random direction, where our theory predicts NO gain; and (ii) a HOMOGENEOUS-SATURATION regime -- a single-domain corpus, or capabilities engineered to co-saturate -- where, with no differential saturation to exploit, our theory again predicts NO gain. A measured gain in EITHER null falsifies the saturation attribution and demotes the result to "mixture tuning helps, mechanism unproven." This is the line between claiming a phenomenon and claiming a cause.

The fifth obstacle is brute cost: the design is a grid over a model-size ladder x mixtures x overtraining ratios, and training every cell at frontier scale is infeasible -- and the scientifically interesting cells (r=40 at 1B-plus parameters) are the most expensive. The instrument is to never train the expensive cells directly. We use a SMALL-MODEL LADDER (70M to 1.4B) with a shared tokenizer and one frozen eval harness, adopt mu-P or fixed-shape model families (Yang et al. 2022; Pythia shapes, Biderman et al. 2023) so hyperparameters transfer cleanly across N, and PREDICT large-r, large-N behavior by extrapolating laws fit at small r and small N rather than measuring it. Compute is spent on the fits and on a SINGLE held-out confirmation point, not on filling the grid; the extrapolation discipline from the second obstacle is what makes spending compute this way defensible rather than reckless.

**[Question 4] - Why hasn't it been solved before?**

The problem sits in a blind spot created because two mature literatures grew along orthogonal axes and never intersected in the regime that matters. The scaling-law literature varies tokens and parameters but holds the mixture and the metric fixed (aggregate loss); the data-mixture literature varies the mixture but holds the regime fixed (compute-optimal) and the metric fixed (aggregate validation loss). The specific cell we target - capability-resolved, mixture-coupled, in the overtrained / inference-optimal regime - is the intersection neither side had a reason to enter.

On the scaling-law side, Kaplan et al. (2020) and Hoffmann et al. (2022) define the frontier in aggregate loss at r=1 (compute-optimal), with no notion of overtraining or per-capability structure. Sardana et al. (2023), "Beyond Chinchilla-Optimal: Accounting for Inference," is the closest prior work: it legitimizes overtraining by adding inference cost to the objective - but it does so with a single aggregate-loss term and a fixed mixture, so it inherits exactly the capability-blindness we attack. It tells you how long to overtrain, never what to overtrain on. Gadre et al. (2024), "Language models scale reliably with over-training and on downstream tasks," connects loss to downstream performance and validates the over-trained regime, which looks adjacent - but, as their own framing makes explicit, the loss-to-downstream reliability they document is established under a fixed mixture; they predict downstream from loss without reallocating the mixture by per-capability saturation, so they observe the coupling precisely in the setting where it holds and never enter the moving-mixture setting where it breaks. The Densing Law line (2024) measures capability density as an empirical trend across released models, but it offers no allocation mechanism and no mixture coupling - it describes that small models are getting denser, not how to spend overtraining tokens to make them denser.

On the data-mixture side, DoReMi (Xie et al., 2023), DoGE, RegMix (2024), Data Mixing Laws (Ye et al., 2024), and DataComp-LM (2024) all optimize the mixture, but every one targets aggregate validation loss in or near the compute-optimal regime and treats the optimal mixture as a single operating point, not a function of how far you overtrain. None fits per-capability saturation laws; none makes the mixture a scheduled function of r; none predicts or tests an aggregate-loss versus downstream-capability dissociation. Data Mixing Laws predicts loss from mixture proportions but stays in aggregate-loss space; RegMix regresses mixture-to-loss at small scale and extrapolates, again to aggregate loss. They answer "best fixed mixture for low average loss," not "how should the mixture move as overtraining saturates different capabilities at different rates."

The intro also names distillation as the alternative path to capable small models - Phi (Abdin et al., 2024) and sequence-level knowledge distillation (Kim and Rush, 2016). That path changes the training signal (teacher targets or curated "textbook" data) rather than the allocation of a fixed token budget across capabilities as a function of r. It is orthogonal to and combinable with ours: saturation-aware allocation can be applied to a distillation corpus or a teacher-labeled mixture just as well as to raw pre-training data. So distillation does not subsume the question; it is a different lever that our knob composes with.

Stated plainly, the unclaimed quadrant is the triple product {capability-resolved} x {data-mixture-coupled} x {overtrained / inference-optimal regime}. Sardana occupies {aggregate} x {fixed-mixture} x {overtrained}. The mixture-law papers occupy {aggregate} x {mixture-coupled} x {compute-optimal}. Gadre occupies {downstream-aware} x {fixed-mixture} x {overtrained} - it holds two of our three coordinates but keeps the mixture fixed and never uses per-capability saturation to reallocate tokens. We are the first to set all three at once: we (i) resolve the law per capability, (ii) make the mixture an explicit function of r tied to per-capability saturation, and (iii) operate in the overtrained / inference-constrained regime - and we both predict and demonstrate the central dissociation (at matched inference FLOPs and matched total tokens, equal-or-worse aggregate validation loss but better aggregate and worst-case downstream capability). The framework also nests the prior frontier: collapse to one capability bucket and an r-invariant mixture and you recover Sardana, and at r=1 you recover Chinchilla - so we strictly generalize rather than contradict.

Finally, pre-empt the obvious objection - "isn't this just DoReMi run at higher token counts?" No. DoReMi optimizes domain weights to minimize worst-case excess loss relative to a reference, evaluated at compute-optimal scale; its objective is a loss-space minimax over domains, not a capability-saturation-aware allocation, and it produces a single mixture, not a mixture schedule in r. Two things only exist in our regime: the per-capability saturation structure - different capabilities flattening at different token budgets - is invisible at compute-optimal token counts and only emerges deep in overtraining; and the aggregate-loss versus downstream-capability dissociation is a property of that regime, not of DoReMi's compute-optimal operating point. Re-running DoReMi with more tokens still minimizes a loss-space objective on a fixed target and will, by construction, keep pouring tokens into buckets that lower average loss even after their downstream capability has saturated - the exact failure we diagnose. The difference is therefore not "more tokens"; it is a different objective (per-capability saturation under inference cost), a different output (mixture as a function of r), and a different, falsifiable claim (the dissociation).

**[Question 5] - What are the key components of my approach and results?**

Overview and objective. We test one dissociation claim: at MATCHED inference FLOPs (set by model size N, since inference costs ~2N FLOPs per token) and MATCHED total training tokens D, a capability-resolved, r-dependent mixture yields EQUAL-OR-WORSE aggregate validation loss but BETTER aggregate and BETTER worst-case downstream capability than fixed-mixture overtraining. Demonstrating equal-or-worse loss together with better capability is the crux: it shows that aggregate-loss scaling laws -- including the inference-aware single-scalar law of Sardana et al. (2023) -- are capability-blind.

Models. A decoder-only Transformer ladder at {70M, 160M, 410M, 1.0B, 1.4B} non-embedding parameters, using Pythia-style shapes or trained from scratch under mu-P, with an optional 2.8B confirmation point. Because inference FLOPs ~2N, "matched inference budget" is simply matched N, which keeps the serving-cost axis clean and controllable.

Data sources (controllable mixture axes). Four corpora whose proportions p sum to 1: general web (DCLM-baseline / SlimPajama / RefinedWeb-like), code (The Stack v2 / StarCoder data), multilingual (mC4 / CulturaX, with FLORES-aligned languages), and math/reasoning-heavy (OpenWebMath / Proof-Pile-2 / arXiv). The mixture p over these four sources is the optimization variable. The Stack v2 and StarCoder will be filtered against their published opt-out lists and screened for credential-like patterns; mC4 and CulturaX will apply the upstream deduplication and language-ID filters as released.

Overtraining ratios. r = D / D_Chinchilla in {1, 2, 5, 10, 20, (40)}, with the Chinchilla-optimal ~20 tokens/parameter as the r=1 anchor (Hoffmann et al. 2022). For a 1B model, D_Chinchilla ~ 20B tokens, so r=20 ~ 400B tokens.

Capability evaluations (held-out). Knowledge: TriviaQA, Natural Questions (closed-book). Multilingual: FLORES-200, XNLI, multilingual MMLU; multilingual results are reported disaggregated by language-resource tier (high / mid / low) using FLORES-200 as the primary coverage instrument, given that multilingual MMLU is weighted heavily toward high-resource languages. Math/reasoning: GSM8K, ARC-Challenge. Code: HumanEval (pass@1), MBPP. Commonsense: HellaSwag, WinoGrande. We additionally report aggregate validation loss / perplexity on a held-out general corpus, specifically to exhibit the dissociation. Benchmarks are never used to fit the scaling laws; only a small disjoint split is used to fit the loss-to-accuracy link.

The fitted laws. Per-capability saturation law L_c(N, D, p) = E_c + A_c / N^alpha_c + (B_c / D^beta_c) * exp(sum_d t_{c,d} * p_d), which nests the Chinchilla N,D power law and the Data Mixing Laws exponential-in-proportions form (Ye et al. 2024). A monotone loss-to-accuracy link f_c maps each L_c to a predicted benchmark accuracy Acc_c_hat (sigmoidal fit on held-out splits; cf. Gadre et al. 2024, Ruan et al. 2024). B1 collapse (provable): a single capability bucket plus an r-invariant mixture (all t_{c,d} = 0) reduces this family to the inference-aware single-scalar law of Sardana et al. (2023), which itself reduces to Chinchilla at r=1 -- so prior work is the corner case of ours.

Procedure. (1) Train the ladder across the (N, r, p) grid at small N and small-to-moderate r, logging per-capability loss and benchmark accuracy. (2) Fit the per-capability laws L_c(N,D,p), the aggregate loss law, and the links f_c; validate by held-out extrapolation (fit r <= k, predict r > k) with bootstrap CIs and by synthetic-injection calibration. (3) Solve the inference-aware allocation: for a held-out target (N*, r*), find p*(r) that maximizes the aggregate objective sum_c w_c * Acc_c_hat (and, separately, the worst-case objective min_c Acc_c_hat) subject to sum_d p_d = 1, p_d >= 0, and p_multilingual >= p_floor (the multilingual corpus proportion at r=1, serving as an explicit low-resource-language fairness floor), at fixed N* (matched inference FLOPs) and fixed D* = r* * D_Chinchilla(N*) (matched tokens) -- this mixture-as-a-function-of-r is the core artifact. (4) Train the predicted capability-resolved model at (N*, r*) and ALL baselines at the same (N*, r*). (5) Test the dissociation at matched inference FLOPs and matched tokens by comparing aggregate val loss against aggregate and worst-case downstream capability across arms.

Baselines. Chinchilla-optimal (r=1, fixed mixture) as the compute-optimal anchor; uniform overtraining on a fixed mixture at r* (the Llama-3 small-model recipe); Sardana inference-aware (aggregate-loss-optimal token budget, fixed mixture); DoReMi (Xie et al. 2023) and RegMix (Liu et al. 2024) mixtures (optimized for compute-optimal / aggregate loss, not r-resolved); distillation (Phi-style sequence-level KD, Abdin et al. 2024, from a larger teacher) at matched inference budget, which tests the alternative path named in the source paper; and the two PLACEBOS (random-partition reallocation and homogeneous-saturation), both predicted to show no gain.

Metrics. PRIMARY: per-capability accuracy and aggregate downstream score (mean over clusters). WORST-capability score (min over clusters), capturing balance/fairness across skills. Aggregate validation loss / perplexity, reported to demonstrate the equal-or-worse-loss-but-better-capability dissociation. Tokens-to-saturation per capability (read off the fitted laws), the mechanism quantity. Inference-FLOP-matched Pareto frontier (aggregate or worst-case capability versus serving cost ~2N), the deployment-relevant object. Extrapolation error of the fitted laws (predicted versus measured at held-out r), which calibrates trust in the entire apparatus.

Ablations. Per-capability objective versus aggregate-loss objective (isolates the value of capability resolution). r-dependent mixture versus r-fixed mixture (isolates the value of letting the mixture move with overtraining). With versus without the distillation term in the objective. Partition granularity (5 clusters versus coarser or finer). Sensitivity to the saturation functional form (exponential-in-p versus alternative Data-Mixing-Law parameterizations).

Expected outcome and honest negative. We expect a measurable dissociation: at matched N and D, the capability-resolved model should show equal or slightly worse aggregate val loss yet a higher aggregate downstream score (target effect on the order of a few points) and a notably higher worst-capability score; and we expect p*(r) to TILT toward slow-saturating capabilities (reasoning, code) as r grows, while fast-saturating capabilities (commonsense, parts of knowledge) are de-emphasized in the overtraining tail. Honest negative: if there is NO dissociation at matched budget (capability tracks aggregate loss), OR if EITHER placebo shows a gain, the central claim fails and the contribution degrades to a careful negative result -- a calibrated diagnostic of WHEN single-scalar scaling laws (aggregate or inference-aware) are sufficient and when capability resolution is actually needed, which is itself useful guidance for practitioners overtraining small models.
